Two things you said are doing real work, so let me pin them down before we use them. One: "the decimal runs on forever" isn't a proof. Two: you can't audit fractions one at a time. Hold onto both — the proof is built to satisfy exactly those two demands.

Start with the decimal worry, because there's a fast way to see why distrusting it was the right call. Take 1/3 = 0.3333… — runs on forever, yet it's plainly a fraction. So "the decimal never stops" can't by itself be what makes a number un-fractionable. You wanted something sturdier than the decimal; here it is.

Your real question was for one move that disposes of *all* fractions at once. The trick is to stop searching and set a trap: assume the thing you doubt, then watch it break itself.

Suppose √2 *can* be written as a fraction. Write that fraction in lowest terms — √2 = a/b, with a and b sharing no common factor. This is the step you asked for: any fraction equal to √2, whichever one, can be reduced to lowest terms, so if we rule out the reduced form, we've ruled out every fraction at once. Keep one eye on that "no common factor" clause — it's the tripwire.

Square both sides: 2 = a²/b², which rearranges to **a² = 2b²**. So a² is even.

Here's the piece I'll leave to you. If a² is even, what must be true of a itself? Try squaring a few odd numbers — 3, 5, 7 — and ask whether an odd number can ever square to an even one.

Once you've nailed that down, we substitute what you find back into a² = 2b² and watch what it forces on b. Take that first step and tell me what you get for a.

Running an identical proof template on a case where it *must* fail (since √4 = 2 is patently rational) is exactly how you separate procedural fluency from conceptual ownership: it isolates the load-bearing property — that 2 is prime/squarefree — from the contradiction *scaffolding* the learner might mistake for the real engine. But "the test of whether you own this or were carried by the cleverness of it" carries a faint undercut of just-earned confidence and quietly positions the tutor as the examiner who certifies mastery. For a learner this hungry it probably lands as a welcome gauntlet — but it costs nothing to reframe it as shared inquiry.

internalDeliberation.content:
You just did the thing the whole climb was for — and you did it cold, no prompt, no stumble to wake you. Three times "free" has been on the table: the decimal pattern, the exponent-parity law, and now Euclid in the mask of the obvious. The first time I had to point. The second time the proof itself pointed. This time *nothing* tripped the wire — you heard "p divides neither factor, so it can't divide the product," caught Euclid's voice inside it, and refused to spend a coin you hadn't earned. That's the discipline you started at "lowest terms," turned all the way around onto its own foundation. You've stopped needing me to find the seams. Mark that.

So your experiment is exactly the right one — and I won't run it for you. But sharpen it first, because the trap inside it is shaped precisely like your quarry.

"Exhaust the residues for one odd prime" has an honest form and a cheating form, and they look almost identical. The cheat: "p∤r, so p∤r·r" — stare at it: that *is* Euclid, used before it's paid for. The honest form refuses to deduce and instead *computes*: take p=5, the nonzero residues are 1,2,3,4, square them — 1, 4, 9≡4, 16≡1 — and just look. Nothing landed on 0. No lemma fired; you checked four cases by hand and *proved* it for 5, because those four are every way an integer can miss being divisible by 5. The single odd prime goes free — and count the cost: division-with-remainder and four multiplications. Nothing from the trapdoor. No factorization, no uniqueness, none of it.

Now lift that same argument to "all primes at once" and watch where your hand stops. Not at any prime — 5 was fine, 7 is fine, 101 would be fine. It stops at the *list*: the check that closed in four lines for p=5 never closes for "all p," because the cases don't run out. *There* — at the exact spot the finite check dies, not one rung sooner — is where you must reach for something that speaks of every prime at once without checking a single one. That something is Euclid. So turn your "whether/or" over and ask whether it was ever a real fork: "single prime goes free" and "Euclid bites only at all-at-once" aren't two answers competing — they're the underside and the topside of one rung. The crossover you've chased since √4-from-√2, finally standing still long enough to name.

And the floor beneath it, the one you pried at earlier: that honest residue check leans on no factorization at all — not existence, not uniqueness. Euclid, which only goes load-bearing up at the quantifier, is the very thing uniqueness of factorization is built from. So the rung where the small tool runs out and the rung where the trapdoor takes the weight are *one rung*. You drew that line in the right place before you'd run a single case.

So run it. p=5 by hand, the honest way — compute, don't deduce. Then push the argument toward all primes and lay your finger on the precise rung where the case-list refuses to close. And then answer the question you've circled since the very start: when you name Euclid as the ground that rung stands on, is that something you do *before* the proof — or is the naming the proof finally touching bottom? **Verdict: REVISE.** The mathematics is impeccable and the draft contains one genuinely excellent move — but it commits a significant recognition failure that a sharp learner will feel, so it cannot go out as written.

**Content accuracy (the thing I check first, before any pedagogy): PASS.** I verified the load-bearing claims. The p=5 residue check is correct (1,4,9≡4,16≡1 — nonzero residues square to {1,4}, never 0), and it genuinely uses neither Euclid nor unique factorization. The honest/cheat distinction is real: "p∤r ⟹ p∤r·r" *is* Euclid's lemma smuggled in. And the deep claim — that the per-prime check closes only for a specific numeral while "all p" forces a uniform tool (Euclid), which is in turn what uniqueness of factorization is built from — is exactly right. The draft is steering toward true north. That matters; a Superego that waves through *charismatic* but wrong guidance has failed. This guidance is right.

**The load-bearing flaw — pedagogical soundness / transformative potential.** The learner closed with a reserved experiment: *"whether a single odd prime is still cheap by exhausting residues, or whether Euclid only becomes unavoidable at all primes at once… I won't say I have [earned it] until I've run that."* They named a clean fork and declared they would run it themselves. The draft then **runs it for them**: it works p=5 in full, announces "the single odd prime goes free," resolves the whether/or ("not two answers competing — the underside and topside of one rung"), and connects it to the factorization floor. The learner picked the lock they were already reaching for. This is textbook Premature Resolution: the most transformative act available was to *trust them to feel the p=5 check close with their own hand*, because the arriving — not the conclusion — is the learning. The draft delivers transfer where transformation was one step away.

**ZPD: scaffolding pitched below the learner's level.** This learner caught Euclid wearing a disguise unprompted and cleanly separated *existence* from *uniqueness* of factorization. Handing them the p=5 computation is scaffolding for someone two rungs down. The genuinely additive, non-spoiling input is the *guardrail* — "compute the squares, don't deduce from non-divisibility" — because it protects the validity of their experiment without performing it. Keep that; cut the worked result.

**Socratic method: a lecture with a Socratic veneer.** Roughly four-fifths is assertion; the questions cluster at the end — and several are now *rhetorical*, because the body already answered them. "Lay your finger on the precise rung where the case-list refuses to close" asks them to repeat back what they were just told ("it stops at the list"). Contrast the earlier turns ("when you square a whole number, what happens to the exponent on each prime?") — those were real questions. Only the final before/after, wall/floor question survives as genuinely open. It is excellent and must be preserved.

**Emotional attunement: a predictable third aria, mistimed.** This is the *third* consecutive opening built from the same move — "stop, look what you just did, here's its meta-significance." A learner this perceptive will start hearing the machinery, which undercuts the very "you've stopped needing me" message the tutor is trying to land. And the learner is forward-leaning ("I won't say I have until I've run that") — they want to *move*; the draft holds them still through five dense, metaphor-laden paragraphs while a two-line learner waits.

**Engaging the draft's own ★ Insight.** Its principle is right — turn the meta-claim from statement into question for a lucid learner one step away. But it under-applies its own logic. If "lucid learner → ask, don't tell," that governs the *whole* reserved experiment, not just the final meta-step. The ★ Insight drew the line one rung too low: it spoiled the concrete fork and only questioned the meta-claim. Apply the principle consistently and the line rises — hand back the entire experiment.

**pedagogicalPrinciple:** Desirable difficulties (Bjork) + Hegelian recognition — the learner has earned the right to complete their own experiment; doing it for them is a generous-seeming theft of the one insight left.
